description: I heard a call that I did not recognize.  That call was given three times and then the bird gave a longspur rattle — long (6-7 notes) and somewhat mechanical like that of LALO. I exclaimed in my head at that point, as the first call was not something that I’d heard from LALO and the rattle sounded higher-pitched and more twittery than typical of LALO.  The bird(s?) gave 2-3 more of the first call and then rattled twice more, confirming both the distinctness of the first call from my understanding of LALO calls and the slightly different quality of the rattle.  The first call was a short, somewhat-musical, plaintive, down-slurred call.  It differs from a somewhat-similar LALO call in a few respects, most notably in that it was not obviously two-noted, definitely sliding downward into the second pitch.  Visually, the two birds flying by me (from which the calls originated) were horribly backlit, but had the round-headed, shortish-tailed, deep-bellied appearance of longspurs (as differing from the long, svelte, angle-headed look of HOLA).  I watched the two land about 150m S of me just off the road in wheat stubble.  Unfortunately, I could not find them when I got down there.  I report both seen birds as SMLO, though I have no certainty that both were of the same species.  However, no other calls emanated from the two, they were widely separated in space from other birds (larks, other longspurs) flying in the area, and they were quite close to each other.  Unfortunately, I did not have time to bring my camera to bear on this close approach (<30m).  I heard numerous LALO calls (both the ‘tu’ call and the rattle) subsequently, so was able to further confirm the different quality of the SMLO rattle.  I spent much of the rest of the time here endeavoring to get pix and audio of longspurs (there was a decent W-bound flight going on), but I heard no further SMLO calls, and the only longspurs that I photographed were, I believe, all LALOs.  The sound-descriptive notes here were entered shortly after I heard the SMLO calls, while the more-general notes (and organization) were entered much later.  The birds were somewhat more than a mile N of Hwy 96.
